topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The campus bulletin board felt like a surveillance network. Every flyer, every notice tacked onto the peeling green board, seemed to whisper the same chilling message: "Final Notice: Operations Cease Summer Term." I kept glancing over my shoulder, convinced someone was watching from behind the ivy-covered library. The silence in the quad, usually a comforting hum of student chatter, now felt heavy, expectant. I started checking the locks on my dorm room door every hour, the metallic click of the deadbolt sounding unnervingly loud in the late afternoon stillness. My roommate, bless her oblivious soul, just thought I was anxious about finals. I just knew, deep down, that this wasn't about grades; this was about erasure. I started hoarding my textbooks, stacking them in the closet until the wood groaned under the pressure, a futile attempt to build a physical barrier against an invisible tide washing over everything familiar.

[story 2]

"Did you see the email?" I asked Sarah, my voice tight, my knuckles white where I gripped my lukewarm coffee mug. She just blinked, scrolling through her phone with a casual air that grated on my nerves. "Yeah, I saw it. It’s official." I didn't look at her, instead focusing intently on the condensation tracing lines down the windowpane. The lights in the common room seemed too bright, exposing every shadow in the corners. I kept replaying the administrator’s monotone announcement in my head, dissecting every syllable for hidden threats. If the building was closing, what else was being taken? Who was coordinating this exodus? I found myself tracing the pattern of the floor tiles with my eyes, searching for anomalies, for signs that the structure itself was compromised, that the foundation was shifting beneath my feet. A tremor, maybe? Or just my own nerves playing tricks on me.

[story 3]

The administrative office smelled overwhelmingly of stale paper and impending doom. I sat across the desk from the dean, my posture rigid, my gaze fixed on the polished wood grain of the conference table. Every time the dean shifted in his seat, I flinched, a tiny, involuntary jerk of my shoulders. He spoke in measured tones about restructuring, about fiscal necessity, but the words felt like coded threats, layers of bureaucratic language designed to obscure a stark reality. I kept tapping my pen against my thigh, a rapid, insistent rhythm that I hoped would somehow anchor me to the present moment, to the tangible reality of the office, rather than the abstract dread swirling in my chest. I noticed the security guard pacing near the doorway, his footsteps slow and deliberate, a shadow moving just beyond the reach of the fluorescent lights. He wasn't looking at the paperwork; he was looking *through* it, assessing the perimeter.
